original_abstract:
While isomerism is a fundamental and ubiquitous concept in organic chemistry, it is exceptionally rare in the field of covalent organic frameworks (COFs). In this work, we report the first controllable synthesis of topological isomers in three-dimensional (3D) COFs by utilizing a unique tetrahedral building unit. By manipulating the solvent environment, we successfully isolated two distinct isomers featuring either a **dia** or **qtz** net (designated as JUC-620 and JUC-621, respectively). Their precise structures were elucidated using a combination of powder X-ray diffraction and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy. 

Our findings reveal that these architectural isomers possess remarkably different porous characteristics. Specifically, JUC-621 (the **qtz** net) features permanent mesopores of approximately 23 Å and a high surface area of ~2060 m² g⁻¹, significantly outperforming JUC-620 (the **dia** net), which is microporous with 12 Å pores and a surface area of 980 m² g⁻¹. These structural differences translate into superior functional performance: the mesoporous JUC-621 demonstrates highly efficient dye removal and an exceptional iodine adsorption capacity of 6.7 g g⁻¹—roughly 2.3 times the uptake of its microporous counterpart, JUC-620 (~2.9 g g⁻¹). This study establishes a novel strategy for engineering COF isomers, expanding the structural diversity and practical utility of these crystalline materials.
humanized_abstract:
Isomerism, which is a basic concept in organic chemistry, is rarely observed in covalent organic frameworks (COFs). Here, we demonstrate the controllable synthesis of topological isomers in 3D-COFs by using a tetrahedral node. By choosing an appropriate solvent, we are able to isolate two isomers of COFs based on a **dia** or **qtz** net (named as JUC-620 and JUC-621, respectively). The exact structures of JUC-620 and JUC-621 were confirmed by powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM).

Comparative Sorption Behaviors of COF Architectural Isomers Herein, two intriguing porous COFs named JUC-621 and JUC-620 were successfully prepared by using **qtz** and **dia** as the net-work frameworks, respectively. According to the detailed pore characterization data, the porous properties exhibited by these two porous COFs are quite different: JUC-621 has permanent mesopores of 23 Å and a surface area of up to 2060 m² g⁻¹; whereas JUC-620 shows microporous structures with pore size of about 12 Å, a surface area of 980 m² g⁻¹. Further assessment indicated that JUC-621 possesses excellent adsorption performance for dye solution, accompanied with an impressive iodine uptake of 6.7 g g⁻¹, which is almost 2.3 times that of JUC-620 (2.9 g g⁻¹). This study introduces for the first time an adjustable approach to design COF architectural isomers at will, which would benefit for designing new functional materials via controlling their structures.

document.output:

Comparative Sorption Behaviors of COF Architectural Isomers Herein, two intriguing porous COFs named JUC-621 and JUC-620 were successfully prepared by using **qtz** and **dia** as the net-work frameworks, respectively. According to the detailed pore characterization data, the porous properties exhibited by these two porous COFs are quite different: JUC-621 has permanent mesopores of 23 Å and a surface area of up to 2060 m² g⁻¹; whereas JUC-620 shows microporous structures with pore size of about 12 Å, a surface area of 980 m² g⁻¹. document.input:
While isomerism is a fundamental and ubiquitous concept in organic chemistry, it is exceptionally rare in the field of covalent organic frameworks (COFs). In this work, we report the first controllable synthesis of topological isomers in three-dimensional (3D) COFs by utilizing a unique tetrahedral building unit. By manipulating the solvent environment, we successfully isolated two distinct isomers featuring either a **dia** or **qtz** net (designated as JUC-620 and JUC-621, respectively). Their precise structures were elucidated using a combination of powder X-ray diffraction and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy. 

Our findings reveal that these architectural isomers possess remarkably different porous characteristics. Specifically, JUC-621 (the **qtz** net) features permanent mesopores of approximately 23 Å and a high surface area of ~2060 m² g⁻¹, significantly outperforming JUC-620 (the **dia** net), which is microporous with 12 Å pores and a surface area of 980 m² g⁻¹. These structural differences translate into superior functional performance: the mesoporous JUC-621 demonstrates highly efficient dye removal and an exceptional iodine adsorption capacity of 6.7 g g⁻¹—roughly 2.3 times the uptake of its microporous counterpart, JUC-620 (~2.9 g g⁻¹). This study establishes a novel strategy for engineering COF isomers, expanding the structural diversity and practical utility of these crystalline materials.
